text: The highlight
Hands down
To make all the difference
The icing on the cake
A saving grace
Above all
To boil down to
A defining moment
To cherish
A keepsake
Bittersweet
The essence
It made all the difference
It all boils down to the people
Above all, I cherish that memory
What I remember most is the people, not the places.
It was the harbor that stayed with me.
It was Liv who showed me the real town.
The thing that made the trip was one quiet afternoon.
What I remember is the welcome.
It was the coffee that made the morning.
It was an old woman who welcomed me.
The reason I travel is moments like that.

You know, people always ask me about the mountains on that trip. But honestly, it was not the mountains that stayed with me. What I remember most is that little kitchen where we cooked with your neighbors.
Exactly. The scenery was the icing on the cake, but it was that kitchen that made the whole week. What I cherish most is that nobody was in a hurry.
And it was your neighbor, the old fisherman, who made all the difference. Do you remember? The trip could have been ordinary. He was its saving grace.
He was, hands down. Come to think of it, the whole trip boils down to that one evening on his porch. That was the defining moment for me.
I still have the little wooden bird he carved for me. It is my favorite keepsake. Above all, that is what those weeks were really about, is it not? Not the views. The people.
It really is. Leaving was bittersweet, but that is the essence of a good trip. What you take home is not a photo. It is a face, a table, an evening you will never forget.
What I remember most is the people.
It was the food that made the trip.
It was the people who made all the difference.
Above all, it is a trip I cherish.
In the end, it all boils down to the people.

Hi Marcelo, it is Liv. You asked me what really made my favorite trip, and I have been thinking about it all evening. And here is the strange part. It was not the scenery, though the mountains were beautiful. What made that trip was a single afternoon. What I remember most is sitting on a stranger's porch, drinking coffee I could not pronounce, while an old woman told me about her whole life in a language I barely understood. It was that hour, hands down, that stayed with me. The essence of the trip was not any place at all. It was the feeling of being welcomed by someone who owed me nothing. Above all, that is what I cherish. Come to think of it, the highlight was never on my list. It just happened. That is the thing about travel, is it not. What you plan is rarely what matters most. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, what we actually remember about our travels, and why it is almost never what we photographed. Here is a question. Think of your favorite trip. Now, what is the first thing that comes to mind. For most people, it is not the famous monument they queued two hours to see. What comes to mind is a meal, a conversation, a moment of kindness from a stranger. That is the great irony of travel. What makes the trip is rarely the thing on the postcard. It is the small, unplanned moment that turns out to be the essence of the whole journey. Psychologists have a name for this. We remember peaks, single vivid moments, not averages. So the lesson is simple. Do not just collect sights. Leave room for the afternoon that boils down to everything. Because when you get home, and someone asks what made the trip, what you will talk about is a person, a table, a porch. And that, hands down, is the part worth travelling for.
The highlight
Hands down
To make all the difference
The icing on the cake
A saving grace
Above all
To boil down to
A defining moment
To cherish
A keepsake
Bittersweet
The essence
What I remember most is the people.
It was the harbor that stayed with me.
It was Liv who welcomed us.
The reason I went back was the people.
What I loved was the silence.
Above all, it is a trip I cherish.
It was the food that made the trip.
It was the people who made all the difference.
In the end, it all boils down to the people.
